story_shape: the freak who bet on himself -- a possible No. 1 pick who ran it back to prove the one thing he hasn't: that he can throw it

plan.spine: a 242-pound athlete with first-round buzz turned down the draft to come back -- because the legs are elite and the passing isn't yet, and a new Air Raid coordinator is here to fix it
blend: forwent the 2026 NFL Draft despite No. 1-overall hype
2025: 2,437 pass yds, 13 TD / 8 INT; 270 rush yds, 5 TD on a 4-8 team
new OC Kendal Briles brings an Air Raid to unlock him
plan.payoff: elite as a runner, raw as a passer -- and he came back to close that gap
plan.forward_stake: whether the passing finally catches up to the athleticism -- the difference between a project and the No. 1 pick

hook: LaNorris Sellers could have been a first-round pick. Instead he's running it back at South Carolina -- because everyone can see the 242-pound athlete, and almost nobody has seen him throw it consistently yet. That's the bet of his 2026.
expand: He entered 2025 with No. 1-overall buzz and left it with questions: 2,437 passing yards, 13 touchdowns against eight interceptions, 270 yards and five scores on the ground -- on a team that went 4-8. The tools are undeniable (6-3, 242, the inevitable Cam Newton comparison), and he bet on them, forgoing the 2026 NFL Draft to come back. South Carolina answered by hiring Kendal Briles to install a free-flowing Air Raid built to unlock exactly his kind of athlete from a too-structured scheme. The forward stakes are the leap that defines his career: the legs are already elite, so 2026 is about the throws -- cleaner pocket play, fewer sacks, more consistency. Do that, and the No. 1-pick conversation comes back. Don't, and he's the cautionary tale of a freak who never learned to pass.
style: Sellers is a problem as a runner and a project as a passer, and the data draws the line hard. On the ground he's elite: our play-by-play grades his rushing success rate in the 91st percentile and his EPA per carry in the 81st -- a 242-pound back playing quarterback. Through the air it's the opposite: an 17th-percentile EPA per dropback, a 22nd-percentile completion rate, and a sack rate in the 2nd percentile -- he holds the ball and takes a brutal number of hits. Scouts (Pro Football Network) see the same film: "wicked arm strength," but he "gets stuck staring down his primary target" and turns throwaways into negatives, "wholly too inconsistent" with accuracy that runs "hot-and-cold." The encouraging note is ball security -- he's "relatively patient" and protective for an elite rushing threat. The whole season is one question: can the passing rise to meet the athlete?
style_layers_pending: PBP QB percentiles woven in (canonical id 8280); fan-vs-rival discourse descriptors pending discourse-extraction cleanup
